title: 1 Chronicles 29
subtitle: Offerings for the Temple; David’s Prayer; Solomon Enthroned

sections.header: Verses 1–9 — David and the Leaders Give Generously

refs.note: The free-will offering for the tabernacle. The pattern repeats: God’s people give freely for the construction of God’s dwelling.
“God loves a cheerful giver.” The NT principle of joyful giving has its roots in this passage.

notes.note: Joy in giving teaches that true generosity is not sacrifice but delight. When giving feels like loss, something is wrong in the heart. When it feels like worship, the heart is right. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: darkĕmōnîm — Persian darics, first minted under Darius I (c.515 BC). Their appearance in a Davidic-era narrative is the most commonly cited anachronism in Chronicles, confirming post-exilic composition. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: David gives from his personal treasury: 3,000 talents of gold and 7,000 talents of refined silver. Then he asks: “Who is willing to consecrate themselves to the LORD today?” The leaders respond with 5,000 talents of gold, 10,000 darics, 10,000 talents of silver, 18,000 talents of bronze, and 100,000 talents of iron. “The people rejoiced at the willing response of their leaders, for they had given freely and wholeheartedly to the LORD. David the king also rejoiced greatly.” The offering is voluntary, joyful, and extravagant.

heb.paragraph: The Hebrew qōḏeš (holiness) denotes separation for divine purpose. In the context of temple worship, it marks the distinction between common and sacred — a boundary that defines Israel's entire liturgical life. The Chronicler's emphasis on holiness reflects his concern for proper worship as the foundation of national life.

notes.note: The Chronicler's portrait of David emphasizes his role as the ideal theocratic king — the man after God's own heart who established worship, organized the Levites, and prepared for the temple. David's reign points typologically to Christ, the Son of David, who perfectly fulfills every dimension of the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7:12-16; Rom 1:3).

The Chronicler's perspective consistently emphasizes God's faithfulness to His covenant promises despite Israel's unfaithfulness. This dual theme — human failure and divine grace — is the heartbeat of all Scripture and finds its ultimate resolution in the cross of Christ, where God's justice and mercy meet perfectly (Rom 3:25-26).

The Chronicler wrote for a post-exilic community needing to understand their identity and hope. His message remains vital: God's promises to David are irrevocable, worship is the center of covenant life, and faithfulness — not circumstances — determines blessing. Believers today can take the same comfort: our God is faithful even when we are not (2 Tim 2:13).

sections.header: Verses 10–30 — David’s Doxology; Solomon Enthroned; David Dies

heb.gloss: Yours, O LORD, is the greatness
heb.paragraph: David’s doxology (v.11): “Yours, O LORD, is the greatness and the power and the glory and the majesty and the splendour.” Five divine attributes ascribed in a single sentence. This becomes one of the most quoted prayers in Jewish and Christian liturgy — Jesus’ Lord’s Prayer doxology (“for yours is the kingdom and the power and the glory”) echoes it directly.

refs.note: The Lord’s Prayer doxology: “For yours is the kingdom and the power and the glory forever.” A direct echo of David’s prayer.
David’s death in Kings is brief and political. The Chronicler’s version is devotional and liturgical.

notes.note: “Yours is the greatness” teaches that all praise is recognition, not creation. We do not make God great by praising him; we acknowledge the greatness that already is. Worship is the perception of reality.

“We have given you only what comes from your hand” is the death of all religious pride. If everything we offer to God was first given by God, then our generosity is simply God’s generosity returning to its source. We are channels, not origins.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: This verse is the source of the traditional doxology appended to the Lord’s Prayer in Matt 6:13 (in many manuscripts): “for yours is the kingdom and the power and the glory.” The verbal parallels are unmistakable.
“A second time” (shēnît) refers to a second anointing ceremony — the first was the private/emergency anointing in 23:1; this is the public, full ceremony.

hist.context: David’s prayer before the whole assembly is the theological climax of 1 Chronicles: “Yours, O LORD, is the greatness and the power and the glory and the majesty and the splendour, for everything in heaven and earth is yours.” He acknowledges that the offerings came from God first: “Everything comes from you, and we have given you only what comes from your hand.” Then Solomon is enthroned “a second time” (the first was 23:1), acknowledged by all leaders, and prospers. David dies “at a good old age, having enjoyed long life, wealth and honour.” The book ends not with exile but with worship, generosity, and orderly succession.

notes.note: Solomon's wisdom was a direct gift from God, granted in response to humble petition rather than selfish ambition (cf. James 1:5). Yet even Solomon's legendary wisdom proved insufficient apart from sustained obedience. The truly wise king whom Solomon foreshadows is Christ, in whom 'are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge' (Col 2:3).

Solomon's reign — in both its glory and its eventual decline — serves as a living parable of the danger of spiritual compromise. The wisest man who ever lived was undone by disobedience to clear commands of Scripture (Deut 17:16-17). If Solomon could fall, no believer dare presume upon their own strength. Sustained faithfulness requires sustained dependence on God's grace.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThey gave toward the work on the temple of God five thousand talents and ten thousand darics of gold, ten thousand talents of silver, eighteen thousand talents of bronze and a hundred thousand talents of iron.ESVThey gave for the service of the house of God 5,000 talents and 10,000 darics of gold, 10,000 talents of silver, 18,000 talents of bronze and 100,000 talents of iron.NIVWith all my resources I have provided for the temple of my God\u2014gold for the gold work, silver for the silver, bronze for the bronze, iron for the iron and wood for the wood, as well as onyx for the settings, turquoise, stones of various colors, and all kinds of fine stone and marble\u2014all of these in large quantities.ESVSo I have provided for the house of my God, so far as I was able, the gold for the things of gold, the silver for the things of silver, and the bronze for the things of bronze, the iron for the things of iron, and wood for the things of wood, besides great quantities of onyx and stones for setting, antimony, colored stones, all sorts of precious stones and marble.

themes.note: Offerings for the Temple; David’s Prayer; Solomon Enthroned

coaching.tip: David's final prayer — 'Everything comes from you, and we have given you only what comes from your hand' — reframes all human generosity as returning God's own gifts. Giving is not sacrifice; it is acknowledgment. The theology of stewardship begins with this prayer.

prayer_prompt: David's prayer acknowledged that everything given to God came from God first. Consider asking God about your own giving — have you remembered that you're returning what was His to begin with?
